reference_answer: Castro & Company, LLC, a small business of Alexandria, Virginia.
Contracts Management Enterprises, LLC (CME), a small business of Leesburg, Virginia.
9531BP25Q0010 (request for quotations No. 9531BP25Q0010).
The Federal Election Commission (FEC), for financial management and accounting support services.
Edda Emmanuelli Perez, General Counsel.
Gabriel Fry (for the protester, Castro & Company, LLC); Uri R. Yoo, Esq., and Alexander O. Levine, Esq. (GAO Office of the General Counsel).
November 13, 2025.
April 25, 2025 (issued by email to seven potential vendors).
June 2025.
June 17.
July 2023; CME provides contract specialists, and a CME contract specialist provides direct support to the contracting officer, who was the source selection authority for the protested procurement.
Within 60 days after receipt of the decision, submitted directly to the agency (4 C.F.R. § 21.8(f)).
(1) acquisition-support order to CME (July 2023); (2) solicitation issued (April 25, 2025); (3) selection notice to Castro (June 17, 2025); (4) protest filed, after a requested debriefing.
Castro: Marginal; CME: Outstanding.
Castro: Moderate Confidence; CME: High Confidence.
(1) technical approach (significantly more important than price); (2) price (more important than past performance); (3) past performance.
AR, Exh. 9, Summary of Award at 3-4.

"[s]imple statements of compliance (i.e. 'understood,' 'will comply') without a detailed description of how compliance will be achieved will not be considered sufficient evidence that the proposed services can technically meet the requirements."

GAO found the SOW did not include any requirement for timelines (RFQ at 8-12); the solicitation listed "[t]imeliness" (not timelines) as a methodology element, and Castro's quotation addressed timeliness (AR, Exh. 4 at 12).

Castro's argument that the agency unreasonably assessed a deficiency for key personnel lacking experience; abandoned because the protester did not substantively address the agency's response in its comments.

Three grounds: (1) the OCI consideration was unreasonable and inadequately documented; (2) the technical approach evaluation was unsupported and inadequately documented; and (3) the best-value tradeoff decision was unreasonable and inadequately documented.

Castro's lowest-priced quotation (rated "marginal," not "unacceptable") was excluded from the documented tradeoff without explanation; the solicitation did not define the term "deficiency," and a marginal rating did not render a quotation ineligible for award.
